content.problemStatement:
Chef has a string SSS with length NNN and integer KKK. Let's consider all strings that can be obtained by erasing KKK consecutive characters from the original string ― specifically, for each iii (1≤i≤N−K+11 \le i \le N-K+11≤i≤N−K+1), let f(i)f(i)f(i) be the string that is obtained by erasing the characters at positions i,i+1,…,i+K−1i, i+1, \ldots, i + K - 1i,i+1,…,i+K−1.

Consider a permutation PPP of the integers 111 through N−K+1N - K + 1N−K+1. You need to find the lexicographically smallest permutation PPP such that f(Pi)≤f(Pi+1)f(P_i) \leq f(P_{i+1})f(Pi​)≤f(Pi+1​) for each iii (1≤i≤N−K1 \le i \le N - K1≤i≤N−K).

Since the length of this permutation can be too large, you should only compute its hash: ∑i=1N−K+1Pi⋅i\sum_{i=1}^{N-K+1} P_i \cdot i∑i=1N−K+1​Pi​⋅i modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^7
S contains only lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains two integers N and K (1 ≤ K < N). The second line contains a string S of length N consisting of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the value ( Σ_{i=1}^{N‑K+1} P_i · i ) mod 1 000 000 007, where P is the lexicographically smallest permutation of {1,…,N‑K+1} that makes the sequence f(P_1), f(P_2), … non‑decreasing. f(i) is the string obtained from S after deleting the K characters starting at position i (1‑based).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: f(1)f(1)f(1) and f(2)f(2)f(2) are both "a", the lexicographically smallest permutation is P=[1,2]P = [1, 2]P=[1,2]. The answer is 1⋅1+2⋅2=41 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 41⋅1+2⋅2=4.
Example case 2: f(1)f(1)f(1) is "cde", f(2)f(2)f(2) is "ade", f(3)f(3)f(3) is "abe", f(4)f(4)f(4) is "abc". The lexicographically smallest valid permutation is P=[4,3,2,1]P = [4, 3, 2, 1]P=[4,3,2,1] and the answer is 4⋅1+3⋅2+2⋅3+1⋅4=204 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 4 = 204⋅1+3⋅2+2⋅3+1⋅4=20.

solution.approach: For each i (1 ≤ i ≤ N‑K+1) define f(i) = S[0..i‑2] + S[i+K‑1..N‑1]. The required permutation is obtained by sorting the indices i by the pair (f(i), i). The second key i guarantees the smallest possible permutation when several f(i) are equal. To compare two f(i) and f(j) efficiently we use a polynomial rolling hash of the original string S. With the prefix hash we can obtain the hash of any substring in O(1). The hash of a prefix of f(i) of length L is either completely inside the left part, completely inside the right part, or a concatenation of both; this can be computed in O(1). Using binary search on L we find the longest common prefix of f(i) and f(j) in O(log N) and then compare the next character. Sorting M = N‑K+1 indices therefore costs O(M log M · log N). After sorting we compute the required hash Σ P_i·i modulo MOD. The algorithm runs in O(N + M log M log N) time per test case and O(N) additional memory for the prefix hashes.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) to build prefix hashes + O((N‑K+1)·log(N‑K+1)·log N) for sorting.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the prefix hash array and O(N‑K+1) for the index list.
